topic: Memory - Drop Flags and Conditional Drop

turns.content: I'm reviewing a script that uses std::mem::ManuallyDrop to guard against conditional initialization, but I noticed the compiler is still inserting drop flags in MIR even though the value was never fully moved. The code creates an option-like struct where fields are only initialized if a flag is set, yet the optimizer seems to treat it as fully owned regardless of that state.

You've hit on a subtle interaction between partial moves and drop flags in MIR. When you write code like this: let x = Foo::new(); if cond { use(x); } else { }, the compiler tracks whether each field has been initialized via drop flags. If a struct contains a partially-moved value, those fields remain valid but their ownership state isn't fully resolved until the move completes. The issue you're seeing likely stems from using ManuallyDrop incorrectly or misunderstanding how the drop flag propagates through nested types. Specifically, when you manually suppress dropping with ManuallyDrop, you must ensure the entire struct is treated as unowned in MIR to avoid spurious drop elaboration. If your code initializes fields conditionally without wrapping them in a proper Option<T> or similar pattern that tracks initialization state explicitly, the optimizer may assume full ownership prematurely.
